source.note: Generated exactly from the approved markdown handover; row text is not rewritten.

products.Notes: Full taxonomy. Transcript + clinical + competitive evidence.
PDP + listing brief evidence. Limited live creative proof.
PDP + listing brief. Live creative evidence thin.
11-sheet product doc. Persona mapping. Clinical data strong.
Website + kit page. No standalone product doc.
Kit PDPs + reviews. Inherits from component products.
Website copy. Inherits from RCF + LPP.
Website only. No product doc. Needs creative validation.
Live. Product doc exists. Limited creative evidence so far.
Website copy only. No standalone doc.

rows.One-line definition: Previous tools failed because they used the wrong mechanism — not because the consumer gave up too soon.
rows.What this angle conveys: Your failure was the system's fault. RCF is a different mechanism, not more of the same.
rows.When to use: Consumer has tried SA face washes, derm visits, peels, or product stacks without success.
rows.Do not use when: Consumer is new to acne care and has no prior failure.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Testimonials naming specific failed products. Competitive comparison vs SA.

rows.MA Name: Clears Acne. Won't Destroy Your Skin.
rows.One-line definition: Ceramides, allantoin, panthenol, Cica — the formula treats without stripping the barrier.
rows.What this angle conveys: Control without damage. You don't have to choose.
rows.When to use: Consumer afraid of harsh actives or has had barrier damage before. C4 (sensitive) + C2 (burnt out).
rows.Do not use when: Consumer who wants the strongest possible treatment with no barrier concern.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Testimonials on skin feeling comfortable. Ingredient callout statics.
rows.Notes: Merged old MA-R2 + MA-R3 + MA-R6 + MA-R11.

rows.One-line definition: First visible signs within days — not proof you're cured, but proof you're not wasting time again.
rows.What this angle conveys: Reassurance that it is working before full clearing lands.
rows.When to use: MOF-BOF. Consumer is skeptical and needs an early win to stay consistent.
rows.Do not use when: Consumer already convinced — too much reassurance creates doubt.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Testimonials describing specific early drying or calming moments.

rows.One-line definition: Smaller BPO particles penetrate deeper, work faster, cause less surface irritation.
rows.What this angle conveys: Formulation innovation that explains why this is different from any BPO they may have tried before.
rows.When to use: TOFU + MOF. Any consumer open to understanding why this works differently.
rows.Do not use when: Consumer allergic to BPO or with strong objection to it — address barrier safety first.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: India's 1st credential. Micronised particle size explanation.
rows.Best-fit formats: Founder video, AA video, ingredient static

rows.One-line definition: Double-blind, split-face, UKAS-accredited study. 78% clearer in 6 weeks. Visible reduction from Day 4.
rows.What this angle conveys: Objective evidence — not just self-reported. Tested on Indian skin at an independent lab.
rows.When to use: MOF-BOF. Evidence-seeking consumer who has been burned by unproven claims.
rows.Do not use when: Cold TOF audiences before emotional buy-in is established.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Study method card. Stats creative. CLM-005.
rows.Best-fit formats: Data static, proof static, AA video

rows.One-line definition: Leave it on for 2 minutes and it stops being a cleanser — it becomes a treatment step.
rows.What this angle conveys: Time on skin is the mechanism. You're not doing more. You're just letting it work.
rows.When to use: Any audience that has dismissed the face wash format.
rows.Do not use when: Consumer who already believes BPO works — preaching to the converted.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Founder/AA explanation of short-contact therapy. Demo of application + 2-min wait.
rows.Best-fit formats: Founder video, AA video, demo video

rows.One-line definition: For acne that tracks your cycle, spikes with stress or travel, or never goes away. This was built for that pattern.
rows.What this angle conveys: You don't have to choose between oral medication and giving up.
rows.When to use: MOST IMPORTANT MA. C1 + C7 + C8 primary. PCOS, hormonal, cyclical, overnight stress breakouts.
rows.Do not use when: Consumer whose acne is purely comedonal / texture — wrong mechanism.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Testimonials on oral medication trap. Cyclical acne proof.
rows.Notes: Covers hormonal, PCOS, stress, overnight, postpartum.

rows.One-line definition: Apply, leave 2 minutes, rinse. That contact time is what converts a cleanser into a treatment.
rows.What this angle conveys: How you use it is why it works. The protocol is the product.
rows.When to use: MOF consumer who has the product or is about to buy. Education + onboarding.
rows.Do not use when: Cold TOF — too instructional before emotional buy-in.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Demo video. Sink-side routine. AA explaining contact time.

rows.MA Name: How to Use It Without Wrecking Your Skin
rows.One-line definition: Tingling and initial dryness are normal. Here's the 2-step and 3-step protocol to support your skin through adjustment.
rows.What this angle conveys: Adjustment is expected — not damage. Stopping early is the mistake, not the tingling.
rows.When to use: Onboarding + MOF. Post-purchase reassurance. Pairs with BRGM cross-sell.
rows.Do not use when: TOF cold audiences before purchase intent exists.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Protocol card. Testimonials on managing initial adjustment.

rows.One-line definition: Named actives that soothe and protect while BPO treats — for the ingredient-educated consumer.
rows.What this angle conveys: The formula was designed to protect skin, not just treat acne.
rows.Do not use when: Consumer who finds ingredient talk overwhelming — use MA-02 instead.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Ingredient spotlight statics. AA explainer.

rows.Belief Name: I was using the wrong tool — my skin isn't the problem
rows.Belief shift: from → to: FROM: "My skin is just difficult / I haven't tried hard enough." TO: "The category gave me the wrong mechanism. My effort was real. The tool was wrong."
rows.One-line definition: Shifts self-blame to category accountability.
rows.Do not confuse with: Not general product cynicism — this is mechanism-specific.
rows.Notes: Primary belief for C2 and C8.

rows.Belief Name: My acne treatment doesn't have to destroy my skin to work
rows.Belief shift: from → to: FROM: "Strong treatment = peeling, dryness, damage — pick one." TO: "Treatment and barrier protection coexist in this formula."
rows.One-line definition: Effective ≠ harsh.
rows.Do not confuse with: Not a gentle skincare claim — ceramides are doing real barrier work.

rows.Belief Name: If my skin is calming down early, it's working
rows.Belief shift: from → to: FROM: "I can't tell if this is working / if nothing happens fast I'll stop." TO: "Pimples drying and calming in the first few days is real evidence — not just hope."
rows.One-line definition: Redefines what "working" looks like before full clearing.
rows.Do not confuse with: Not about promising fast results — about interpreting early signals correctly.

rows.Belief Name: One right product beats five wrong ones
rows.Belief shift: from → to: FROM: "More products = more chances of fixing it." TO: "The problem was never effort or quantity. It was mechanism fit."
rows.One-line definition: Precision over volume.
rows.Do not confuse with: Not about minimalism for comfort — about targeted intervention replacing a failed stack.

rows.Belief Name: A face wash can treat my acne — if it's designed to stay on
rows.Belief shift: from → to: FROM: "Face washes clean. They can't treat acne." TO: "A face wash designed for 2-minute contact time stops being a cleanser and becomes a treatment step."
rows.One-line definition: Format credibility — the 2-minute contact time is the mechanism.
rows.Do not confuse with: Not the same as B-04 which is about routine simplification. This is about format capability.

rows.Belief Name: My hormonal acne is manageable, not something I have to live with forever
rows.Belief shift: from → to: FROM: "My hormonal acne is a condition I'm losing to — it'll always come back." TO: "Hormonal/cycle/stress-triggered acne follows a pattern. That pattern is treatable and predictable."
rows.One-line definition: Control is possible — not cure, but management.
rows.Do not confuse with: Not about curing hormonal imbalance — about managing the skin manifestation of it.
rows.Notes: Primary for C1 + C7. Critical MA-07 belief.

rows.Belief Name: Initial tingling or dryness doesn't mean I should stop
rows.Belief shift: from → to: FROM: "Tingling / mild dryness = this product is too harsh for me." TO: "Skin adjusting to active treatment looks like this. It's expected. I know how to support it."
rows.One-line definition: Adjustment reactions are normal — stopping early is the mistake.
rows.Do not confuse with: Not about pushing through pain — about distinguishing normal adjustment from actual damage.
rows.Notes: Pairs with BRGM cross-sell. From Protocols doc.

rows.Simple definition: Adult women with cyclical, cystic, painful acne tied to hormones or PCOS.
rows.How to identify: Mentions period flares, cystic acne, chin/cheek concentration, hormonal imbalance.
rows.Core pain / desire: "My acne hurts and it comes back every cycle no matter what I do."
rows.Do not confuse with: C02 — C01 can name their trigger. C02 cannot.

rows.Simple definition: Years of trying everything — dermats, actives, brands. Resigned, not just frustrated.
rows.How to identify: Mentions 2+ years, multiple products/dermats failed, stopped expecting results.
rows.Core pain / desire: "I've spent years and money trying to fix this. Nothing has worked."
rows.Do not confuse with: C01 — C02's primary emotion is exhaustion. C01's is pain.

rows.Simple definition: Usually clear skin. Sudden breakout before an important event.
rows.How to identify: Mentions upcoming wedding, shoot, interview, or event. Not a chronic acne sufferer.
rows.Core pain / desire: "My skin was fine and now I have a breakout right before something important."
rows.Do not confuse with: C01 — C03 is situational, not chronic.

rows.Simple definition: History of harsh product reactions — peeling, stinging, barrier damage. Scared to try anything new.
rows.How to identify: Mentions prior damage from actives, extreme sensitivity, fear of trying again.
rows.Core pain / desire: "Every time I treat my acne, something goes wrong. I'm scared to try again."
rows.Do not confuse with: C02 — C04's fear is skin damage. C02's fear is product failure.

rows.Simple definition: Teenager or early 20s. Acne is new. Wants simple, won't stick to complex routines.
rows.How to identify: Young, mentions acne starting recently, easily overwhelmed.
rows.Core pain / desire: "My skin just started breaking out and I don't know where to start."
rows.Do not confuse with: C03 — C05 is new to acne entirely, not reacting to a single event.

rows.Simple definition: Doesn't identify as a skincare person. Wants one thing that works. No routine.
rows.How to identify: Mentions not wanting skincare, wanting something simple, male framing.
rows.Core pain / desire: "I don't do skincare. I just want my face to stop breaking out."
rows.Do not confuse with: C05 — C06 actively resists complexity. C05 is just new to it.

rows.Simple definition: Skin changed after pregnancy, breastfeeding, or stopping contraception. Disoriented.
rows.How to identify: Mentions postpartum, stopping pill, skin changing after a life event.
rows.Core pain / desire: "My skin never used to be like this. Since my pregnancy/pill change it's completely different."
rows.Do not confuse with: C01 — C07's trigger is a life event. C01's is a chronic pattern.

rows.Simple definition: Everyone — mom, relatives, friends — has given them a home remedy. Tried all of it. Done listening.
rows.How to identify: Mentions ghee, haldi, soap, neem, or unsolicited advice from family.
rows.Core pain / desire: "Everyone told me something different. I tried all of it. None of it worked."
rows.Do not confuse with: C02 — C08's fatigue is from cultural home remedies. C02's is from product trials.

rows.Simple definition: Wasn't looking for an acne solution. A reel stopped the scroll. Something felt different.
rows.How to identify: Mentions not actively searching, stumbling on an ad or reel, gut instinct purchase.
rows.Core pain / desire: "I wasn't looking for this. A reel stopped me. Something felt different about this one."
rows.Do not confuse with: C03 — C09 had no urgency or event. They weren't searching.

rows.Simple definition: A wedding, shoot, interview, or important day is coming up and a pimple appeared.
rows.Trigger evidence: Mentions event + timeline.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-RCF-02 — SD-01 has an external deadline.

rows.Simple definition: Period approaching or just started. Predictable breakout arrived on schedule.
rows.Trigger evidence: Mentions period timing, cycle pattern.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-RCF-01 — SD-02 has no external event.

rows.Simple definition: Tried a new face wash, serum, or active. Skin worsened or reacted badly.
rows.Trigger evidence: Mentions switching products + worsening.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-RCF-07 — SD-03 is a self-chosen new product. SD-07 is ongoing derm treatment.

rows.Simple definition: Someone pointed out their acne. The comment is the breaking point.
rows.Trigger evidence: Mentions a specific comment from someone else.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-RCF-08 — SD-04 has an external trigger. SD-08 is purely internal.

rows.Simple definition: Skin was clear for weeks or months. Acne came back.
rows.Trigger evidence: Mentions having had clear skin + relapse.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-RCF-03 — SD-05 happened without changing anything.

rows.Simple definition: New city, job, college, marriage, climate change. Skin changed because life changed.
rows.Trigger evidence: Mentions life change + skin change.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-RCF-02 — SD-06 is a one-time event, not a cycle.

rows.Simple definition: On a derm prescription, retinoid, or acid. Skin reacting — red, dry, peeling.
rows.Trigger evidence: Mentions current active treatment + sensitivity.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-RCF-03 — SD-07 is expected treatment sensitivity. SD-03 is a mistake.

rows.Simple definition: No specific event. Daily mirror confrontation has become a source of distress.
rows.Trigger evidence: Mentions checking skin constantly, daily fixation.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-RCF-04 — SD-08 is internal. SD-04 was triggered by someone else.

rows.Simple definition: Was on steroids or antibiotics. Just stopped. Knows skin may rebound.
rows.Trigger evidence: Mentions stopping oral medication + fear of what comes next.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-RCF-07 — SD-09 is about stopping meds. SD-07 is about ongoing sensitivity.

rows.Simple definition: Came across a review, reel, or testimonial. Someone like them got clear skin.
rows.Trigger evidence: Mentions seeing a review or testimonial that stopped the scroll.
rows.Do not confuse with: C09 (Accidental Discoverer) — that is who they are. This is what just happened.

rows.Proof / Claim: Visible reduction in acne in just 4 days
rows.Can be used for: AA/FO verbal. LEV end card super ONLY.
rows.Required context / caveat: In-vivo clinical study, UKAS-approved independent laboratory, 2025. Individual results may vary.
rows.Do not say: "Cleared in 4 days" / "4 days and you're done"
rows.Notes: Same data as CLM-02. Never use both in same asset.

rows.Can be used for: AA/FO verbal. LEV end card super ONLY.
rows.Notes: Same 4-day data as CLM-01. Never both in same asset.

rows.Can be used for: AA/FO ONLY. NEVER LEV in any format.
rows.Notes: Never appears in consumer testimonial in any form.

rows.Proof / Claim: Prevents breakouts for 78% clearer skin in 6 weeks
rows.Can be used for: AA/FO verbal. LEV end card super ONLY.
rows.Notes: Exact wording: "78% clearer skin in 6 weeks".

rows.Proof / Claim: 47% reduction in active acne in 1 week
rows.Notes: From study data in Amazon listing.

rows.Proof / Claim: India's first face wash with Patented Micronised BPO

rows.MA Name: Hide It While You Heal It
rows.One-line definition: Invisible film treats the pimple underneath while you go about your day. Two jobs, one product.
rows.What this angle conveys: You don't have to choose between treating and showing up.
rows.Do not use when: Consumer who only wants treatment and has no social/visibility concern.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: UGC showing film + daily life continuation.

rows.One-line definition: Film creates a physical barrier between pimple and dust, pollution, gym equipment, phone screens, pillowcases.
rows.What this angle conveys: Your environment is making it worse. The patch stops that.
rows.Do not use when: Indoor-only consumers with no environmental concern.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Demo: dust/pollution/phone contact blocked.
rows.Notes: Separate from MA-01 — environment is different from social visibility.

rows.One-line definition: Foundation, concealer, and setting spray go over it without lifting, cracking, or peeling.
rows.What this angle conveys: Full-face look, no compromise, pimple being treated simultaneously.
rows.When to use: TOF-MOF. Makeup wearers with active breakout.
rows.Do not use when: Bare-skin users, men.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Swipe demo: makeup over patch, no lifting.

rows.One-line definition: Liquid film adapts to any shape, size, and location. Sticker patches leave gaps.
rows.What this angle conveys: Works where sticker patches can't — nose fold, jawline, cluster, irregular shape.
rows.When to use: MOF. Consumer frustrated by sticker patches.
rows.Do not use when: Consumer who has never used any patch format.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Close-up: liquid-to-film demo on irregular pimple.

rows.One-line definition: Physical barrier between pimple and hands, phone, pillow at night. Healing doesn't require willpower.
rows.What this angle conveys: You don't have to resist the urge. The patch creates the barrier.
rows.When to use: TOF-MOF. Pickers, accidental touchers, night contact.
rows.Do not use when: Consumer who genuinely doesn't touch their face.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Honest testimonials on accidental contact. Skit/relatable format.
rows.Notes: Covers accidental physical contact including sleep.

rows.One-line definition: Less irritation during healing = less PIH left behind. How you heal determines what you're left with.
rows.What this angle conveys: Treating it right now is the first step in preventing marks.
rows.When to use: MOF-BOF. Consumer anxious about post-pimple marks.
rows.Do not use when: Consumer with no marks concern — wrong entry.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Testimonial + visual showing healed pimple with less residual marking.
rows.Notes: Note: LPP doesn't treat existing marks. It protects the healing process to reduce future marks.

rows.Belief Name: I can treat my pimple and still show up — at the same time
rows.Belief shift: from → to: FROM: "If I treat it, it shows. If I cover it, I'm not treating it." TO: "An invisible film does both — treatment underneath, presentable outside."
rows.One-line definition: Format capability: treat and appear simultaneously.
rows.Do not confuse with: Not about denial — the patch is doing real healing work.

rows.Belief Name: My pimple keeps getting worse from accidental contact — not just because of acne
rows.Belief shift: from → to: FROM: "I'm not touching it." TO: "Fingers, phone, pillow, makeup brushes cause most pimple damage accidentally."
rows.One-line definition: Accidental contact is a bigger factor than people realise.
rows.Do not confuse with: Not about making people feel guilty — about removing the need for willpower.

rows.Belief Name: A liquid film fits my pimple. A sticker can't.
rows.Belief shift: from → to: FROM: "A pimple patch is a pimple patch — they're all the same." TO: "Liquid conforms to every shape and location. A sticker leaves gaps on anything that isn't flat."
rows.One-line definition: Format superiority vs sticker patches — specific and visual.
rows.Do not confuse with: Not a general "liquid is better" claim. Specifically about fit.

rows.Belief Name: How I heal my pimple determines what I'm left with after
rows.Belief shift: from → to: FROM: "Marks are just what happens after a pimple." TO: "PIH is influenced by how much the pimple was irritated during healing. Protect it properly, reduce what's left."
rows.One-line definition: Healing quality affects mark outcome.
rows.Do not confuse with: Not a marks-treatment claim. LPP does not treat existing marks.

rows.Belief Name: I'm paying ₹5+ per sticker patch when I could be paying ₹2
rows.Belief shift: from → to: FROM: "Pimple patches are all roughly the same price." TO: "300+ patches in one tube at ₹2 per patch vs ₹5+ for sticker patches."
rows.One-line definition: Value: dramatically more patches per rupee spent.
rows.Do not confuse with: Not a "buy cheap" message — positioned as better value for a superior format.

rows.Belief Name: My patch is completely invisible under makeup — not just barely there
rows.Belief shift: from → to: FROM: "Pimple patches show under makeup — people can see it." TO: "This forms a transparent film that makeup goes over seamlessly. No one can see it."
rows.One-line definition: True invisibility under makeup — not a compromise.
rows.Do not confuse with: Not about hiding acne — about not announcing that you're treating it.

rows.Simple definition: Regular makeup user with an active pimple needing simultaneous treatment and coverage.
rows.How to identify: Mentions makeup use + active pimple on same day.
rows.Core pain / desire: "I have a pimple and I need to cover it but also need it to not get worse."
rows.Do not confuse with: LPP-C02 — C01 is a regular day, no event deadline.

rows.Simple definition: Usually clear skin. Something important is happening tomorrow. Pimple appeared today.
rows.How to identify: Mentions specific upcoming event + sudden pimple.
rows.Core pain / desire: "Something important is happening tomorrow and I woke up with this."
rows.Do not confuse with: LPP-C01 — C02 has a specific deadline. Acute urgency.

rows.Simple definition: Gets pimples that leave marks. Wants to protect healing to reduce future marks.
rows.How to identify: History of post-pimple dark marks. Wants to prevent them.
rows.Core pain / desire: "Every time I get a pimple I end up with a mark for months."
rows.Do not confuse with: Note: LPP does not treat existing marks. This cohort is about prevention during healing.

rows.Simple definition: Gets pimples regularly. Wants a reliable spot response as part of how they manage acne — not just for emergencies.
rows.How to identify: Regular acne, not one-off. Mentions "I get them all the time."
rows.Core pain / desire: "I get pimples regularly. I need something I can reach for every time."
rows.Do not confuse with: LPP-C02 — C04 has no urgency or event. This is maintenance.

rows.Simple definition: Touches or picks pimples without thinking — on the phone, while sleeping, when stressed.
rows.How to identify: Mentions picking, touching, waking up having touched it.
rows.Core pain / desire: "I touch my pimples without thinking. I need something that physically stops the contact."
rows.Do not confuse with: LPP-C03 — C05 wants to stop the behaviour. C03 wants to prevent the outcome.

rows.Simple definition: Long days outside, gym, commute, pollution. Pimple exposed to everything.
rows.How to identify: Mentions commuting, outdoors, gym, heat.
rows.Core pain / desire: "I'm out all day and my pimple is exposed to everything."
rows.Do not confuse with: LPP-C02 — C06's concern is environmental, not event-based.

rows.Simple definition: Event tomorrow. Pimple appeared today.
rows.Trigger evidence: Mentions event + pimple + time pressure.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-LPP-02 — SD-01 has a deadline.

rows.Simple definition: Has to be visible to people today. Pimple is there and unavoidable.
rows.Trigger evidence: Working day + active pimple + visibility concern.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-LPP-01 — SD-02 has no deadline. Daily exposure.

rows.Simple definition: On actives and a pimple appeared while skin is sensitised. Can't use strong spot treatments.
rows.Trigger evidence: Mentions actives + sensitive skin + pimple.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-LPP-01 — SD-03 is about skin state, not deadline.

rows.Simple definition: Full-face makeup planned. Has a pimple. Wants to treat and cover simultaneously.
rows.Trigger evidence: Mentions full makeup + pimple + coverage need.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-LPP-02 — SD-04 is specifically about the act of applying makeup.

rows.Simple definition: Long day outside. Pimple needs protecting from the environment all day.
rows.Trigger evidence: Mentions outdoor day + pimple + protection need.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-LPP-02 — SD-05 is environmental. SD-02 is social visibility.

rows.Simple definition: End of day. Alone. Prone to touching/picking at night. Wants a barrier before sleep.
rows.Trigger evidence: Mentions night, picking, wanting to protect during sleep.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-LPP-03 — SD-06 is behavioural/nighttime. SD-03 is skin sensitivity.

rows.Notes: Use as positioning credential, not standalone angle.

rows.Required context: State as fact. ₹2 per patch calculation.
rows.Do not say: Any exact comparison unless pricing is verified.
rows.Notes: Verify current pricing before using cost comparison.
Scope of clinical test needs verification.

rows.One-line definition: Active acne gone but marks remain. That's the second problem — and it needs its own solution.
rows.What this angle conveys: Getting rid of acne was step one. This is step two.
rows.When to use: TOF-MOF. Consumer who has cleared or is clearing acne but has visible PIH.
rows.Do not use when: Consumer with active acne who hasn't reached the marks stage.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Hansika/Pooja on-camera mark journey. Before/after marks.

rows.One-line definition: Actives and derm procedures cleared the acne but left hyperpigmentation behind.
rows.What this angle conveys: You did everything right. The marks are a side effect of treatment, not failure.
rows.When to use: MOF. Consumer on actives who developed PIH.
rows.Do not use when: Consumer whose marks came from acne directly, not treatment.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Testimonials from consumers on active treatment regimens.

rows.One-line definition: The mark journey as a visible timeline. You will see it working before it's done.
rows.What this angle conveys: Progress is observable from the first few days — you don't have to trust blindly.
rows.When to use: MOF-BOF. Consumer impatient with marks treatments.
rows.Do not use when: Consumer wanting overnight results — frame realistically.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Hansika's on-camera mark progression description.

rows.Belief Name: My acne clearing doesn't mean my skin is solved — the marks are a separate battle
rows.Belief shift: from → to: FROM: "Once my acne clears, my skin is solved." TO: "Clearing acne and fading the marks it left are two completely separate battles."
rows.One-line definition: The second chapter after acne — marks need their own intervention.
rows.Do not confuse with: Not creating a new problem — naming a gap that already exists.

rows.Belief Name: My PIH marks aren't permanent — they respond to the right treatment
rows.Belief shift: from → to: FROM: "PIH just fades over time — there's nothing to do but wait." TO: "Post-acne marks respond to targeted treatment. Waiting is a choice, not the only option."
rows.One-line definition: Marks are treatable, not just time-dependent.
rows.Do not confuse with: Not about making dramatic speed claims. About shifting from passive to active.

rows.Simple definition: Acne has mostly cleared but dark marks remain. Solved one problem, stuck with another.
rows.How to identify: Mentions cleared acne + remaining marks. Recent history.
rows.Core pain / desire: "My acne is mostly gone but the marks are still there. I solved one problem and created another."
rows.Do not confuse with: SFA-C02 — C01's marks are relatively recent.

rows.Simple definition: Has had marks for months or years. Tried things. Nothing moved much. Almost accepted them.
rows.How to identify: Mentions months/years of marks, resignation setting in.
rows.Core pain / desire: "I've had marks for a year. I've tried things. They haven't moved. I've almost accepted them."
rows.Do not confuse with: SFA-C01 — C02's marks are old and stubborn. Closer to resignation.

rows.Simple definition: Acne mostly gone but marks still cause social self-consciousness. Avoids photos, events, being documented.
rows.How to identify: Mentions avoiding photos, filters, events — specifically because of marks, not active acne.
rows.Core pain / desire: "My acne is mostly gone but the marks make me feel like my skin still isn't presentable."
rows.Do not confuse with: SFA-C01 — C03's issue is specifically social confidence, not just frustration.

rows.Simple definition: Active acne cleared or clearing. Marks are now the most visible thing on the face.
rows.Trigger evidence: Mentions clearing acne + marks now prominent.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-SFA-02 — SD-01 is recent/new marks becoming noticeable.

rows.Simple definition: Has been trying or waiting. Marks aren't changing. Resignation setting in.
rows.Trigger evidence: Months of marks + nothing changing.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-SFA-01 — SD-02's marks are old and stuck.

rows.Simple definition: Upcoming photo, wedding, shoot. Marks are why they don't want to be photographed.
rows.Trigger evidence: Mentions upcoming event + mark self-consciousness.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-SFA-01 — SD-03 has an external deadline.

rows.Simple definition: Realised they always use filters or edit photos to hide marks. The pattern itself becomes the trigger.
rows.Trigger evidence: Mentions always filtering / editing / never posting raw.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-SFA-03 — SD-04 is a self-awareness trigger, no upcoming event.

rows.Simple definition: A comment specifically about marks or uneven tone — not active acne — is the breaking point.
rows.Trigger evidence: Mentions a comment about marks or pigmentation.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-SFA-03 — SD-05 already happened. SD-03 is anticipatory.

rows.Proof / Claim: India's first serum with 8% Solubilized Azelaic Acid

rows.Do not say: Any specific % reduction claim without study.

rows.Proof / Claim: Clinically designed to reduce post-acne pigmentation and redness
rows.Notes: Wording must stay within approved scope. Needs User Review on exact claim scope.

rows.MA Name: Kajal, SPF, Makeup — One Step, Everything Off
rows.One-line definition: Kajal, waterproof mascara, SPF — everything off in one step. No second cleanser. No cotton pad. No rubbing.
rows.What this angle conveys: You don't have to fight your makeup to remove it.
rows.When to use: TOF. Lead angle. Kajal-specific proof is most resonant in India.
rows.Do not use when: Consumer who doesn't wear makeup or use SPF.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Demo: kajal application + EMC removal. Clean result.
rows.Notes: Kajal leads — most universally resonant Indian removal proof.

rows.One-line definition: Emollient base, not oil. Removes like a cleansing oil without the comedogenic risk.
rows.What this angle conveys: You don't have to choose between cleansing power and not breaking out.
rows.When to use: TOF-MOF. Acne-prone consumer who has avoided cleansing oils.
rows.Do not use when: Non-acne-prone consumer focused purely on removal.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Testimonial: acne-prone consumer who avoided oils.

rows.MA Name: Double Cleanse — Without the Double Step
rows.One-line definition: Replaces the two-step double cleanse ritual with one product.
rows.What this angle conveys: If you double cleanse, you don't have to anymore.
rows.When to use: MOF. Consumer who knows about double cleansing and wants simplification.
rows.Do not use when: Consumer who has never heard of double cleansing.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Side-by-side: two-step routine vs EMC single step.

rows.MA Name: Too Tired to Make It Complicated
rows.One-line definition: One product, 30 seconds, everything off — even on the lowest-energy days.
rows.What this angle conveys: Removes the friction that causes people to skip removal entirely.
rows.When to use: TOF-MOF. Any consumer who sometimes skips makeup removal because of effort.
rows.Do not use when: Consumer for whom removal is never a problem.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Relatable scenario: tired evening, one quick step.
rows.Notes: Not limited to night — any low-energy moment.

rows.MA Name: Cleanse Right, Everything After Works Better
rows.One-line definition: Sunscreen residue, sebum, makeup sitting on skin undermines every active you apply afterward.
rows.What this angle conveys: Your actives work better when the first step is right.
rows.When to use: MOF. Consumer using actives who isn't seeing expected results.
rows.Do not use when: Consumer not using actives with no routine.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: AA: consequences of inadequate cleansing on active performance.

rows.Belief Name: I can remove everything — kajal, SPF, waterproof makeup — without irritating my skin
rows.Belief shift: from → to: FROM: "I have to choose between removing everything and not irritating my skin." TO: "Emollient base has the solvency without the comedogenic risk. Thorough and gentle are not a trade-off."
rows.One-line definition: Complete removal without compromise.
rows.Do not confuse with: Not about cutting corners.

rows.Belief Name: What I don't remove properly affects how everything works the next morning
rows.Belief shift: from → to: FROM: "Cleansing is just about feeling clean." TO: "SPF residue, sebum, and makeup compromise active treatments applied afterward."
rows.One-line definition: First step quality affects every subsequent step.
rows.Do not confuse with: Not a scare tactic — logical connection between cleansing and treatment efficacy.

rows.Belief Name: Removing my makeup doesn't have to be a ritual I dread
rows.Belief shift: from → to: FROM: "Proper makeup removal is multi-step and effort-intensive." TO: "One product, 30 seconds, everything off — even when you have no energy."
rows.One-line definition: Format removes the friction that causes skipping.
rows.Do not confuse with: Not about cutting corners — about removing the barrier to consistent removal.

rows.Simple definition: Daily makeup user. Scared of cleansing oils because they break out. Never found a remover that works without causing pimples.
rows.How to identify: Daily makeup + acne-prone + oil avoidance.
rows.Core pain / desire: "I wear makeup daily but cleansing oils break me out. Nothing removes properly without causing pimples."
rows.Do not confuse with: EMC-C03 — C01's fear is breakouts. C03's fear is stripping.

rows.Simple definition: Knows about double cleansing. Wants to do it but the two-step ritual is too much.
rows.How to identify: Mentions double cleansing knowledge + wanting simplification.
rows.Core pain / desire: "I know about double cleansing and want to do it properly but the two-step is too much."
rows.Do not confuse with: EMC-C04 — C02 wants simplification. C04 wants active treatment support.

rows.Simple definition: Skin gets tight, red, or uncomfortable after washing. Current cleanser is part of the problem.
rows.How to identify: Mentions post-wash tightness/redness.
rows.Core pain / desire: "My skin gets tight and red after washing. My cleanser is making things worse."
rows.Do not confuse with: EMC-C01 — C03 isn't worried about breakouts. Worried about stripping.

rows.Simple definition: Using RCF, retinoids, or acids. Knows clean canvas matters for actives to work.
rows.How to identify: Mentions active treatment + wanting to support it with better cleansing.
rows.Core pain / desire: "I'm using actives and I want my cleansing step to support them, not undermine them."
rows.Do not confuse with: EMC-C02 — C04 has a specific treatment they're optimising. C02 wants simplification.

rows.Simple definition: Wears SPF 50 daily. By evening, skin has a layer of sunscreen and sebum that their face wash doesn't fully remove.
rows.How to identify: Mentions daily SPF + feeling of residue by evening.
rows.Core pain / desire: "I wear SPF 50 daily and by evening my skin has a layer of residue my face wash can't remove."
rows.Do not confuse with: EMC-C01 — C05 may not wear makeup at all. Removal concern is SPF + sebum.

rows.Simple definition: Full makeup worn today. Needs complete removal tonight.
rows.Trigger evidence: Full makeup day + removal concern.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-EMC-04 — SD-01 is about makeup weight. SD-04 is about format dissatisfaction.

rows.Simple definition: Just started RCF, retinoids, or acids. Realised clean canvas matters.
rows.Trigger evidence: New active + recognition of cleansing importance.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-EMC-03 — SD-02 is proactive before damage. SD-03 is reactive after damage.

rows.Simple definition: Current cleanser causing tightness, redness, or stripping.
rows.Trigger evidence: Post-wash tightness/redness description.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-EMC-02 — SD-03 is damage already done.

rows.Simple definition: Current removal method is inadequate — kajal still on, SPF not gone, cotton pads required.
rows.Trigger evidence: Mentions wipes/micellar + dissatisfaction.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-EMC-01 — SD-04 is about format inadequacy. SD-01 is about coverage weight.

rows.Simple definition: Tired. Every other removal method feels like too much effort right now.
rows.Trigger evidence: Low energy mention + consideration of skipping removal.
rows.Do not confuse with: SD-EMC-01 — SD-05 can happen with minimal makeup. Energy level, not makeup weight.

rows.Proof / Claim: Removes kajal, SPF, and waterproof makeup

rows.Required context: Ingredient fact — emollient base, not oil.
rows.Do not say: "Oil cleanser" — it is NOT an oil cleanser. Emollient base.

rows.One-line definition: New-gen encapsulated filters don't sit in the pore. The reason your last sunscreen caused breakouts was old-gen filters — not SPF itself.
rows.What this angle conveys: You weren't wrong to avoid sunscreen. You were using the wrong formula.
rows.Do not use when: Consumer whose concern is white cast, not breakouts.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Shanthi C review. "First sunscreen that didn't break me out." Demo on acne-prone skin.

rows.One-line definition: Microencapsulated mineral filters. No white or grey cast on any Indian skin tone.
rows.What this angle conveys: Made for Indian skin tones, not adapted for them.
rows.When to use: TOF-MOF. White cast sufferer. Deeper skin tones.
rows.Do not use when: Fair-skinned consumer for whom cast was never a concern.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Demo on deep Indian skin tone. Before/after: old SPF vs Clear Protect.

rows.One-line definition: Fluid gel, blends in seconds, no stickiness, no film, no heaviness — even in Indian heat.
rows.What this angle conveys: Finally a sunscreen you don't have to fight with.
rows.When to use: TOF. Texture hater. Sensory objection entry.
rows.Do not use when: Consumer who has never had a texture problem with sunscreen.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Sensory UGC. Application demo on oily skin. Hansika "ekdum matte look" type.

rows.MA Name: Real SPF, Not Just a Feeling
rows.One-line definition: SPF 57.65 in-vitro tested. PA++++. 6-filter photostable system. Lightweight does not mean weak.
rows.What this angle conveys: Proof that what feels light is still full protection.
rows.When to use: MOF-BOF. Consumer doubting whether a good-feeling SPF actually works.
rows.Do not use when: TOF cold — too technical as entry.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Stats card with study attribution. AA explaining photostable filters.

rows.One-line definition: No pilling, no lifting. Reapplication over makeup works too.
rows.What this angle conveys: Protection that doesn't disturb your look.
rows.When to use: MOF. Makeup wearer who has experienced pilling.
rows.Do not use when: Bare-skin user — irrelevant.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Demo: foundation over sunscreen, no pilling.

rows.One-line definition: Full SPF 50+ protection that doesn't make your skin feel punished for wearing it.
rows.What this angle conveys: You don't have to choose between protection that works and SPF that feels good.
rows.When to use: TOF-MOF. Any SPF avoider. Broad entry.
rows.Do not use when: Consumer already convinced sunscreen feels good.
rows.Primary creative proof needed: Testimonials on feel + protection combined.

rows.Belief Name: SPF used to break me out because of old-gen filters — not because my skin can't handle SPF
rows.Belief shift: from → to: FROM: "Sunscreen breaks me out — SPF just doesn't work for my skin." TO: "Old-gen oil-soluble filters sit in pores. New-gen encapsulated filters don't. Wrong formula, not wrong skin."

rows.Belief Name: Most sunscreens weren't made for Indian skin tones — this one was
rows.Belief shift: from → to: FROM: "Sunscreen always leaves a white cast on my skin." TO: "Microencapsulated minerals with zero cast — formulated for the full range of Indian skin tones."

rows.Belief Name: A sunscreen that feels like nothing can still give me full SPF 50+ protection
rows.Belief shift: from → to: FROM: "If it feels like nothing, it's probably not doing much." TO: "SPF 57.65 tested. PA++++. 6-filter photostable system. Lightweight ≠ unprotected."

rows.Belief Name: I don't have to choose between protection that works and SPF that feels good on my skin
rows.Belief shift: from → to: FROM: "Either it protects or it feels good. Can't have both." TO: "This was designed to be both — fluid gel that blends in seconds with real SPF protection."

rows.Core pain: "Every sunscreen I try breaks me out. I've stopped buying them."
"Sunscreens sting, cause redness, or react badly. I've given up."
"Every mineral SPF leaves a white or grey cast on my skin. SPF isn't designed for me."
"I hate how sunscreen feels — greasy, sticky, heavy. I skip it."
"I need SPF that works as a base and I can reapply over makeup without pilling."

rows.Trigger: Just started RCF/retinoids. Told SPF is now non-negotiable.
Heat rising. Current SPF feels unbearable or they're skipping it.
Tried a sunscreen. Got pimples. Stopped using SPF.
Noticed white cast in photos or natural light. Embarrassing enough to prompt switch.
Mid-day — after gym/outdoors. Current SPF makes reapplication impossible over makeup.
Skin visibly tanned, marks darkened from sun exposure. SPF now urgent.

rows.Proof / Claim: Dermatologically tested on sensitive skin — non-irritant

rows.Do not say: Any efficacy claim beyond filter type.

rows.MA Name: Make Your Acne Treatment Work Better
rows.One-line definition: Barrier damage causes treatments to cause redness and peeling. Repair the barrier and actives become tolerable and more effective.
rows.When to use: MOF. Consumer on RCF or actives. Primary cross-sell angle.

rows.One-line definition: Barrier damage is one reason acne keeps returning. Repair the barrier → fewer future breakouts.
rows.When to use: TOF-MOF. Consumer whose acne keeps coming back.

rows.One-line definition: For reactive skin on actives, weather, or stress — a stable daily layer that prevents overreaction.
Gel texture. Non-comedogenic. Fragrance-free. Barrier repair without oiliness or breakout risk.
rows.When to use: TOF-MOF. Consumer who avoids moisturiser fearing breakouts.

rows.MA Name: Why You Need to Switch Moisturisers
rows.One-line definition: Your current moisturiser hydrates. It doesn't repair. Ceramides + Cica + Niacinamide = different category.
rows.When to use: MOF-BOF. Consumer already using a moisturiser who hasn't seen barrier improvement.

rows.One-line definition: Ingredient-led. Named actives for the ingredient-educated consumer.

rows.Belief Name: My acne treatment works better when my skin barrier isn't damaged
rows.Belief shift: "Without barrier repair, treatments cause dryness and peeling that forces me to stop."

rows.Belief Name: My barrier being broken is part of why my acne keeps coming back
rows.Belief shift: "Repeated harsh cycles damage the barrier, creating conditions for the next breakout."

rows.Belief Name: A moisturiser can repair my skin barrier without breaking me out
rows.Belief shift: "Gel texture + ceramides + Cica — repairs deeply without a pore-clogging ingredient."

rows.Belief Name: My current moisturiser hydrates my skin — but it doesn't repair my barrier
rows.Belief shift: "Hydration and barrier repair are different jobs. Most moisturisers only do one."

rows.Core pain: "I'm on RCF/retinoids and my skin is getting dry and red. I need support."
"My acne cleared but my skin still feels damaged. Dull, flaky, sensitive."
"I have oily/acne-prone skin and I've always skipped moisturiser fearing breakouts."
"I clear my skin, it comes back. I've been in this cycle for years."

rows.Driver Name: Skin Reacting to RCF or Actives
rows.Trigger: A few days into RCF/retinoid. Skin tight, dry, or peeling.
Active phase over. Skin looks dull, feels tight. In repair mode.
Uses moisturiser regularly but still dry. Not doing enough.
Acne gone. Texture remains rough or dull. Wants quality restoration.

rows.Driver Name: Breaking Out After Adding a Product
rows.Trigger: Added a moisturiser or product, started breaking out. Wants non-comedogenic alternative.

rows.Proof / Claim: Reduces skin damage by 70% in 4 weeks
rows.Notes: From website. Attribution and study details required before use in ads. Mark as Needs User Review.

rows.MA Name: No Confusion About What to Pair
rows.One-line definition: This combination is designed to work together. No research needed.
rows.When to use: MOF-BOF. Over-researched consumer paralysed by choice.

rows.One-line definition: RCF clears + BRGM sustains. Each works better than either alone.
Same products, lower cost per unit, delivered as a system.
rows.Notes: Don't use as primary TOF angle.

rows.One-line definition: Three products. One goal. Clear, repair, protect. Nothing else needed.
rows.When to use: TOF-MOF. Over-routinised or first-time routine builder.

rows.Belief Name: Products designed to work together give me better results than products I assembled myself
I don't need to research routine compatibility — this system already figured that out

rows.Core tension: "I want to start treating my acne properly but I don't know which products to buy or combine."
"I've been using one TSS product and seeing results. I want to extend into a full routine."
"I don't want to spend time figuring out if this face wash works with that moisturiser. Just tell me."

rows.Trigger: New to TSS. Wants a complete starting point without research.
Existing customer. Seeing results. Ready to build a fuller routine.
Birthday, festival, self-gifting. Kit format makes natural gift.

rows.One-line definition: RCF reduces the pimple + LPP makes it invisible while it heals. Two jobs simultaneously.
Mini sizes for travel, events, shoots — always have it when acne shows up unexpectedly.
Same formula in 10-15ml. Real results. Before you buy full size, find out if it works for your skin.
Fits in carry-on. Keeps your routine intact wherever you are.
Start with one mini. See the result. Then build the full system.

rows.Belief Name: I can find out if this actually works for my skin before I commit to the full size

rows.Core tension: "I've been burned by products before. I'm interested but not ready to spend full price."
"I need a version of my routine that fits in a carry-on."

rows.Simple definition: Heard about or saw TSS for the first time. Interested but not ready to commit to full size.
Trip coming up. Needs skincare that fits in a carry-on or won't be confiscated.

rows.Driver Name: Wants to Try Before Full Kit
rows.Simple definition: Considering a kit or multi-product purchase. Wants to confirm one product works first.

rows.MA Name: Smoother Skin From the First Wash
rows.One-line definition: AHA + BHA together. Texture, blackheads, rough skin — feel the difference immediately.
Dual-acid formula unclogs pores without destroying the moisture barrier. Non-stripping.
AHA clears surface. BHA goes into the pore. The right tool for comedonal acne specifically.

rows.Belief Name: My skin texture can actually change — I don't have to accept rough skin and blackheads
rows.Belief shift: FROM: "This is just how my skin is." TO: "Texture and blackheads respond to the right formula."

rows.Belief Name: I can clear my pores every day without it irritating my skin
rows.Belief shift: FROM: "Acid cleansers need to be used sparingly or they irritate." TO: "AHA + BHA in a gentle glycerin base = effective and daily-use compatible."

rows.Core tension: "My skin is rough, congested, full of blackheads. Nothing has made it smooth."
"Active acne cleared but texture is still rough and uneven."
"My skin produces too much oil. Pores look enlarged. Blackheads keep forming."

rows.Trigger: Noticed blackheads more than before in mirror/photo.
Foundation doesn't sit smoothly. Rough despite regular routine.
Active acne cleared. Texture remains.
Face getting increasingly oily. Current cleanser not controlling it.

rows.One-line definition: 50% glycerin base. No post-wash tightness, dryness, or redness. Just clean and comfortable.
After every RCF/retinoid/active session — a cleanser that resets without setting back.
Comfortable with daily use — even twice daily on sensitive or reactive skin.
Fragrance-free, alcohol-free, tested on sensitive skin — for skin that reacts to almost everything.

rows.Belief Name: My skin can feel genuinely clean AND comfortable after washing — I don't have to choose

rows.Core tension: "I'm on RCF/retinoid. My current face wash is too harsh for what I'm putting my skin through."
"My skin has been through a lot. Reactive. I want a cleanser that calms it down."
"I use CeraVe. I want something made for Indian skin, Indian humidity, Indian skin tones."

rows.Trigger: Skin tight/uncomfortable after washing. Cleanser is the problem.
Started RCF/retinoid. Previous cleanser now too much.
Not unhappy. Curious about something made for Indian skin specifically.

rows.Name: Video
rows.Definition: Any moving creative — testimonial, demo, skit, founder, AI video, event.

rows.Name: Static
rows.Definition: Any single frame or swipeable sequence — image, carousel, proof card, infographic.

rows.Name: Consumer Testimonial
rows.Definition: Real consumer telling their lived product or problem story. Unscripted personal experience.
rows.Do not use when: Consumer is scripted or acting.

rows.Name: Founder-Led
rows.Definition: Divanshee speaking — intent, accountability, behind the scenes, product rationale.
rows.Do not use when: Another person is presenting as the founder.

rows.Name: Expert / Dermat
rows.Definition: Credentialed dermatologist, skin scientist, or AA archetype on screen.
rows.When to use: Clinical proof, objection handling, ingredient explanation.
rows.Do not use when: No credentials present.

rows.Name: Creator / UGC
rows.Definition: Paid or organic creator. Product in their routine or POV. Not their personal acne history.
rows.Do not use when: Creator is sharing a genuine personal acne story — that is VS1.

rows.Name: Product Demo
rows.Definition: Product-only or product-first video. Application, texture, formula shown. No creator narrating.
rows.Do not use when: A creator is narrating the demo — that is VS4.

rows.Name: Skit / Scenario
rows.Definition: Scripted or acted scene. Character-led, not creator-led.
rows.Do not use when: Genuine unscripted content.

rows.Name: AI Video
rows.Definition: AI-generated or AI-heavy moving creative.
rows.Do not use when: Real human appears.

rows.Name: Event Coverage
rows.Definition: Real-world event, launch, or behind-the-scenes footage.
rows.Do not use when: Not in active production — Low priority.

rows.Name: Single Image
rows.Definition: One hero frame — product, person, skin, headline-led, or product hero.
rows.When to use: Default for all single-image ads including product-only shots.
rows.Do not use when: Multiple frames needed.

rows.Name: Carousel
rows.Definition: Multi-frame swipeable sequence.
rows.When to use: Educational sequence, product range, before/after steps.
rows.Do not use when: Single message.

rows.Name: Before / After
rows.Definition: Visual contrast of two skin or routine states.
rows.Do not use when: No genuine before/after evidence.

rows.Name: Proof Screenshot
rows.Definition: Review, DM, clinical certificate, or testimonial screenshot.
rows.Do not use when: Screenshot is unverified or from unknown source.

rows.Name: Comparison
rows.Definition: Versus competitor, old routine, category myth, or wrong tool.
rows.Do not use when: Competitor is named explicitly — naming not permitted.

rows.Name: Meme / Topical
rows.Definition: Cultural reference or trending format adapted to TSS messaging.
rows.Do not use when: Brand safety concern with the trend.

rows.Name: Educational / Data
rows.Definition: Teaches a concept, protocol, or leads with a clinical number or study result.
rows.When to use: Clinical proof. Ingredient education. Protocol cards.
rows.Do not use when: TOF cold audiences before emotional buy-in.

rows.Name: Ingredient Focus
rows.Definition: Single ingredient or formula spotlight.
rows.Do not use when: Consumer who finds ingredient talk overwhelming.

rows.Name: Offer / Promotional
rows.Definition: Creative whose primary purpose is communicating a price deal, discount, bundle, or limited offer. e.g. "Buy 3 for ₹1599", "Free mini", B2G1.
rows.When to use: Whenever an offer is the lead message.
rows.Do not use when: The offer is incidental — if product benefit leads and offer is only a secondary badge, use SS1.

rows.Name: Skin Visual
rows.Definition: Acne, marks, texture, redness, or clear skin close-up is the first visual.

rows.Name: Before / After
rows.Definition: Visible before/after or split contrast is the first thing shown.

rows.Name: Product In Use
rows.Definition: Product dispensed, applied, or washed off is the first visual.

rows.Name: Text / Super Dominant
rows.Definition: Bold text overlay is what the viewer reads first before anything else registers.

rows.Name: Screenshot / Proof on Screen
rows.Definition: Review, DM, clinical result, or screenshot shown immediately.

rows.Name: Demo Object
rows.Definition: Unusual prop or demo object (permanent marker, dust, makeup) creates immediate curiosity.
rows.When to use: EMC kajal demo, LPP dust protection.

rows.Name: Relatable Scene
rows.Definition: A recognisable setting — bathroom mirror, makeup desk, gym, commute, bedtime.

rows.Name: Face to Camera
rows.Definition: Person looking directly at viewer and speaking. No text, no product — just face.

rows.Name: Pattern Interrupt
rows.Definition: Unexpected visual, unusual framing, hard cut, or meme format.

rows.Name: Pain Named
rows.Definition: Opens by stating the exact frustration or problem.
rows.Tag from: First spoken line or opening subtitle.
rows.Example: "So the pimples never leave my face."

rows.Name: Functional Payoff Open
rows.Definition: Opens with a tangible, observable result already achieved.
rows.Example: "Even after two-three washes, significant result dikha."

rows.Name: Objection First
rows.Definition: Opens with the viewer's doubt, fear, or reason not to believe.
rows.Example: "Another fake product, another fake review."

rows.Name: Curiosity Gap / Secret
rows.Definition: Opens with withheld information — passively incomplete or actively gatekept.
rows.Example: "She stopped the oral medication."

rows.Name: Myth / Comparison / Reframe
rows.Definition: Opens by challenging a belief OR placing two things side-by-side.
rows.Example: "SA targets pores. BPO kills bacteria."

rows.Name: Relatable Scenario
rows.Definition: Opens with a specific lived moment the viewer recognises as their own.
rows.Example: "Gym mein makeup nahi laga sakte — aur face dikhega."

rows.Name: Authority / Credibility
rows.Definition: Opens with expert, clinical, founder, or credentialed proof.
rows.Example: "India's first face wash with micronised BPO."

rows.Name: Social Proof
rows.Definition: Opens with what someone else experienced — unsolicited, specific, credible.
rows.Example: "Thank you for making this magical thing."

rows.Name: Urgency / Panic
rows.Definition: Opens with a deadline, event, or sudden problem.
rows.Example: "Something important is tomorrow and I woke up with this."

rows.Name: Dark Humour / Irony
rows.Definition: Opens with sardonic or comedic framing of a real problem.
rows.Example: "Phone sunta hai — aur kabhi kabhi sahi sunta hai."

rows.Name: Emotional Payoff Open
rows.Definition: Opens with the emotional state change — confidence, freedom, no longer hiding.
rows.Example: "Now I'm completely ready. Let's go. Kab jaana hai batao?"

rows.Name: Price / Value Lead
rows.Definition: Opens with a cost comparison or value statement.
rows.Example: "₹2 per patch. 300+ patches in one tube."

rows.Name: Negative Trigger / Challenge
rows.Definition: Opens with a conditional challenge that activates fear of being left behind.
rows.Example: "If you're not ready for purging, you're not ready to clear your acne."

rows.Name: Pain → Relief
rows.Definition: Starts in discomfort or frustration, ends in relief.

rows.Name: Confusion → Clarity
rows.Definition: Starts not understanding why things aren't working, ends with sense-making.

rows.Name: Skepticism → Belief
rows.Definition: Starts doubtful, ends convinced by evidence or experience.

rows.Name: Fear → Safety
rows.Definition: Starts afraid of a product, category, or outcome, ends reassured.

rows.Name: Shame → Confidence
rows.Definition: Starts hiding, avoiding, or embarrassed, ends showing up.

rows.Name: Resignation → Hope
rows.Definition: Starts with having given up, ends with reason to try again.

rows.Name: Discovery → Decision
rows.Definition: Starts unaware the solution exists, ends ready to act.

rows.Name: Dark Humour → Vindication
rows.Definition: Starts sardonic or self-deprecating, ends with the thing that actually worked.

rows.Name: Loyalty → Advocacy
rows.Definition: Starts already convinced, ends recommending or restocking. Confirmation, not persuasion.

rows.Name: Top of Funnel
rows.Definition: Cold audience. Problem-aware but not solution-aware.
rows.Creative job: Make them feel understood and introduce the solution.

rows.Name: Middle of Funnel
rows.Definition: Solution-aware. Comparing options, building trust, not yet ready to buy.
rows.Creative job: Build credibility. Address objections. Show proof.

rows.Name: Bottom of Funnel
rows.Definition: Warm audience. Ready to buy, needs final push or reassurance.
rows.Creative job: Close. Final proof. Remove last friction.

rows.Name: Cold Conversion
rows.Definition: Cold audience but creative is built to convert directly — enough proof, specificity, and credibility to skip warm-up.
rows.Creative job: Convert cold. High-specificity hook + immediate proof + direct CTA.
rows.Typical proof needed: Strong proof hook + direct testimonial + clear CTA.

rows.Name: Authority Anchor
rows.Definition: Dermatologist, skin expert, scientist, or credentialed voice. Trusted for correctness.
rows.When to use: Clinical explanation, ingredient education, objection handling.
rows.Do not use when: No credentials — mislabelling damages trust.

rows.Name: Lived Experience Validator
rows.Definition: Real consumer who experienced the problem. Trusted for honesty and relatability.
rows.Do not use when: Consumer is scripted in a way that feels inauthentic.

rows.Name: Founder-Operator
rows.Definition: Divanshee. Trusted for intent, accountability, and behind-the-scenes reasoning.
rows.When to use: Brand story. Product intent. Founder POV.
rows.Do not use when: Another person presenting as founder.

rows.Name: Influencer / Creator
rows.Definition: Paid or organic creator. Product in routine or POV. Not a personal acne history.
rows.Do not use when: Creator is sharing genuine personal acne journey — that is LEV.

rows.Name: Situational Relatable
rows.Definition: Creator mirroring a lived everyday moment. Scene-setting, not story-telling.
rows.Do not use when: Deep emotional story — that is LEV.

rows.Name: Brand / No Creator
rows.Definition: AI-generated, product-only, or brand-produced with no human creator.
rows.When to use: Product demos, AI statics, data cards.
rows.Do not use when: A human appears.

rows.Name: Permission / De-risking
rows.Definition: Makes trying feel safe. Reduces fear of starting.
rows.What it does psychologically: Lowers the psychological cost of action.

rows.Name: Reassurance / Anti-Panic
rows.Definition: Calms fear and prevents quitting or over-reacting.
rows.When to use: Post-purchase. Early use. Initial reaction fear.

rows.Name: Belief Reframe
rows.Definition: Shifts a held belief so the product becomes logical.

rows.Name: Demonstration
rows.Definition: Shows the product visibly working.
rows.What it does psychologically: Creates direct sensory or visual proof.

rows.Name: Proof Delivery
rows.Definition: Evidence — before/after, numbers, clinical data, testimonials.

rows.Name: Objection Handling
rows.Definition: Takes the main reason not to buy and neutralises it.
rows.What it does psychologically: Removes the specific blocker to purchase.

rows.Name: Acute Stabiliser
rows.Definition: Meets the consumer in a panic or urgent moment.

rows.Name: Social Proof Loop
rows.Definition: Distributed trust — people like me used this and it worked.
rows.What it does psychologically: Normalises the purchase through peer evidence.

rows.Name: Conversion Close
rows.Definition: Structured to get the buy — offer, direct ask, final push.

rows.Name: Cost / Value Contrast
rows.Definition: Shows the price of not solving vs the cost of solving.

rows.Name: Dark Humour / Irony
rows.Definition: Uses comedy or irony to make the truth land.

rows.Name: Skin-Led
rows.Definition: Skin, marks, texture, or face is the dominant visual element.

rows.Name: Product-Led
rows.Definition: Product shot is the dominant visual element.
rows.Best used for: Product hero, ingredient focus, launch creative.

rows.Name: Text-Led
rows.Definition: Copy or headline is the primary element. Visual supports text.
rows.Best used for: Data cards, proof statements, bold headline ads.

rows.Name: Lifestyle / Scene
rows.Definition: Person or real-life setting dominates. Product may or may not be visible.

rows.Name: Data / Proof Visual
rows.Definition: Numbers, chart, label, or clinical imagery is the hero.

rows.Name: Illustration / AI
rows.Definition: Illustrated or AI-generated visual style.

rows.Name: AI Voiceover Slide
rows.Definition: AI VO read over a closing card. Primary TSS format.

rows.Name: Creator Spoken
rows.Definition: Talent delivers CTA in their own words, in-frame.

rows.Name: Text End Card
rows.Definition: Closing card with text only. No VO.

rows.Name: No Explicit CTA
rows.Definition: Content ends without a direct ask. Awareness or engagement.

rows.Name: Direct Buy
rows.Definition: Clean, direct call to purchase. "Buy now / Shop now / Try now."

rows.Name: Soft Permission
rows.Definition: Low-pressure invitation. "Give it a try / See if it works for you."

rows.Name: Social Proof Close
rows.Definition: Anchors ask to others' results. "She got her skin clear — what about you?"

rows.Name: Problem Restate
rows.Definition: Echoes the hook's pain before the ask. Closes the emotional loop.

rows.Name: Viewer Direct Address
rows.Definition: Speaks directly to the viewer's turn. "Now it's your turn."

rows.Name: Urgency
rows.Definition: Time, stock, or event pressure.

rows.Name: Risk Reversal
rows.Definition: Reduces the cost of trying. "Zero hope — she tried it anyway."

rows.Name: Offer / Deal
rows.Definition: CTA leads with the deal. "Buy 3 for ₹1599 — shop now." "Free mini on orders above ₹499."

rows.Name: Offer / Promotional
rows.Definition: The body communicates a price deal, bundle discount, or limited offer as the primary message.
rows.Example: "Buy 3 for ₹1599 and get a free mini." "B2G1 this week."
rows.Do not confuse with: SPM2 — SPM1 is about a specific deal/promotion. SPM2 is about the general value argument without a time-bound offer.

rows.Name: Price / Value
rows.Definition: The body communicates cost comparison, price per use, or affordability as the primary argument.
rows.Example: "₹2 per patch. 300+ patches in one tube." "Less than your current routine at ₹799."
rows.Do not confuse with: SPM1 — SPM2 has no specific deal or time pressure. It is a standing value argument.

rows.Name: Clinical / Data Proof
rows.Definition: The body leads with study data, lab results, clinical credentials, or specific numbers as primary proof.
rows.Example: "78% clearer skin in 6 weeks. UKAS-accredited study." "SPF 57.65 in-vitro tested."
rows.Do not confuse with: SPM4 — SPM3 is brand/lab-generated proof. SPM4 is consumer-generated proof.

rows.Name: Consumer / Social Proof
rows.Definition: The body leads with consumer testimonials, reviews, star ratings, or before/after results as primary proof.
rows.Example: Review screenshot. "First sunscreen that didn't break me out — Shanthi C, 2nd purchase."
rows.Do not confuse with: SPM3 — SPM4 is what real people said or experienced. SPM3 is what the study or lab found.

rows.Name: Product Education
rows.Definition: The body explains how the product works, what it contains, or how to use it correctly.
rows.Example: "Apply. Leave 2 minutes. Rinse. That's the treatment step." Ingredient breakdown. Protocol card.
rows.Do not confuse with: SPM6 — SPM5 is about the product mechanism. SPM6 is about the consumer's problem.

rows.Name: Problem / Pain
rows.Definition: The body articulates and deepens the consumer's problem — making them feel understood before any solution is offered.
rows.Example: "You've tried SA. You've been to a dermatologist. It keeps coming back."
rows.Do not confuse with: SPM5 — SPM6 has no product explanation. It is purely problem resonance.

rows.Name: Differentiation
rows.Definition: The body shows how this product is categorically different from alternatives — wrong tool, old-gen formula, competitor category.
rows.Example: "SA targets pores. BPO kills bacteria. You were using the wrong tool." "Old-gen filters sit in your pores. Ours don't."
rows.Do not confuse with: SPM3 — SPM7 is about relative difference. SPM3 is about absolute proof numbers.

rows.Reason: Was mixing visual + content in one field. Now separated.
Same consumer fear as MA-R2.
Mechanism proof for MA-02, not separate angle.
Third name for same consumer fear.
Routine simplification is part of "wrong tool" arc.
Cost/derm-fatigue covered in wrong tool arc.
Mechanism proof for makeup angle, not separate.
Positioning credential, not a creative angle.
Outcome of barrier MA, not separate angle.
Same MA as Stops Accidental Damage.
Adhesion is proof point for Fits Any Pimple.
Same consumer state as Cleared Acne Isn't Solved.
Works against the sale. Tells consumer they can't use the product yet.
Barrier cohort belongs in BRGM, not SFARS.
Product hero IS a single image. VT2 covers product-led visual.
Data card is a subtype of educational.
AI is a production method, not a structural type.
New name is self-explanatory.
"N/A" implies missing data. "BRAND" is intentional.
Distinction too thin for consistent tagging.
CTA Format and CTA Message Type are different decisions.
Separated from Static Subtype. Simplified to 6 visual treatments.

rows.Instruction: Update taxonomy.py with all approved codes, names, and definitions from this document.
rows.Notes: Use stable codes as primary keys.

rows.Instruction: Add Visual Hook Type (VH1–VH9) and Content Hook Type (CH1–CH13) as two separate fields.
rows.Notes: Remove or archive old single Hook Type field. Preserve as legacy field if backward compatibility needed.

rows.Instruction: Split into CTA Format (CF1–CF4) and CTA Message Type (CM1–CM7).
rows.Notes: Retire old C1–C8 CTA Style field.

rows.Instruction: Dashboard dropdowns for MA, Belief, Cohort, Driver should filter by selected product.
rows.Notes: RCF dropdowns show RCF-MA-xx only, etc.

rows.Instruction: Static logging form should not require: Creator Archetype, Video Subtype.
Video logging form should include: Video Subtype, Visual Hook Type, Content Hook Type, Emotional Arc, Creator Archetype, Influence Mode, CTA Format, CTA Message Type.
Add AI-Generated: Yes/No as a metadata field on all static assets. Not a subtype.
Each asset should show: taxonomy tags, AD CODE, Drive link, thumbnail/preview, creator/consumer name, performance data.
Replace TSOF with CONV in all dropdown options.
Keep H1–H20, C1–C8, V1–V11 as archived fields in database if needed for backward compatibility. Do not show in active tagging UI.
Every tagged asset should have a Taxonomy Confidence field: High / Medium / Low / Needs Review.
For kit/bundle assets, allow product field to accept a primary product + secondary product.
Add a Claims field per video that references approved claim codes. Flag if claim code is present without required disclaimer.
rows.Notes: Especially RCF-CLM-03 (93% claim) — NEVER for LEV.

rows.Instruction: Add SPM (Static Message Type) as a statics-only field with values SPM1–SPM7. Do not show on video logging forms.
SS9 (Offer / Promotional) added to Static Subtypes. Add to static subtype dropdown.
rows.Notes: Captures deal/bundle/discount creatives as a distinct structural type.

rows.Instruction: CM8 (Offer / Deal) added to CTA Message Types. Add to CTA message type dropdown.
rows.Notes: For CTAs that lead with a price offer or bundle deal.
